1: 'al-tit'hallel be-yom machar — “do not boast about tomorrow.” Hithpael of hallel (H1984, to praise/boast — the same root as hallelujah) applied to the future (yom machar, the day of tomorrow) as the act of claiming tomorrow’s outcomes as if they were already secured. Ki lo' teda' mah-yeled yom — “for you do not know what a day may bring forth.” Yalad (H3205, to bear/give birth) applied to the day: the day gives birth to its events, and those events are not known in advance. The birth-metaphor emphasizes the unpredictability of what tomorrow will produce.
2: yehallalcha zar ve-lo' picha — “let another praise you, and not your own mouth.” Zar (H2114, a stranger, an outsider, one outside the family or community) as the preferred source of praise inverts ordinary expectations: genuine praise comes from those with no stake in flattering you. Nokri ve-lo' sefatecha — “a foreigner, and not your own lips”: the emphasis on the source (“outside”, “foreign”) implies that self-praise is self-serving and therefore unreliable. Third-party evaluation is epistemically more trustworthy than self-assessment.
3: kovedh'even venatle'h hakholem — “a stone is heavy and sand is weighty.” The two standard weights (stone and sand) that are heavy in the physical realm frame the comparison: veka'as 'evil kaved mishnihem — “but a fool’s provocation is heavier than both.” Ka'as (H3708, vexation, provocation, irritation) and 'evil (H191, the fool — specifically the morally arrogant fool) combine to produce a social weight more burdensome than physical loads. The aphorism quantifies the cost of dealing with provocative foolishness.
4: achzariyut chemah ve-sheetef 'af — “wrath is cruel, and anger is overwhelming.” Achzariyut (H394, cruelty, ruthlessness) qualifies chemah (H2534, wrath, hot fury — the burning aspect of anger); shetteph (H7858, flood, overwhelming torrent) qualifies 'af (H639, nose/nostrils as the seat of anger, the physiological expression of anger through flared nostrils). Both forms of anger are destructive. But the superlative follows: umi ya'amod lifnei qin'ah — “and who can stand before envy/jealousy?” Qin'ah (H7068, jealousy/envy — the possessive intense desire that cannot tolerate rivals) exceeds even wrath and anger as the most powerful of the negative passions.
5: tov tokhachat megullah me'ahavah mesuteret — “better is open rebuke than hidden love.” Tokhachat (H8433, rebuke, reproof) that is megullah (H1540, revealed, open, uncovered) exceeds love that is mesuteret (hidden, concealed, H5641). The paradox: hidden love that fails to correct when correction is needed is inferior to open rebuke that risks the relationship for the sake of the person’s genuine good. The wisdom tradition consistently values the truthful act over the pleasant.
6: ne'emanim pitz'ei 'ohev — “the wounds of a friend are trustworthy.” Petzah (H6481, wound, injury) that is ne'emanim (H539, trustworthy, faithful) paradoxically: the wounding action of genuine friendship is reliable precisely because it aims at the friend’s good rather than the friendship’s preservation. Ve-na'tarot neshiqot sone' — “but an enemy’s kisses are lavish and false.” Na'tar (H6280, excessive, lavish, insincere) qualifies the enemy’s kisses (neshiqot): they are abundant precisely because they are cheap — not costly as the friend’s wound is costly.
7: nefesh seva'ah toosah nofeth — “a full soul tramples on honeycomb.” Nefesh seva'ah (a satisfied/full nefesh) is the person whose appetite is already met; for such a person even nofeth (H5317, honeycomb drippings — the most desirable form of honey) is despised. Ve-nefesh re'evah kol-mar matok — “but to a hungry nefesh, even what is bitter is sweet.” The reversal: the hungry person finds bitterness sweet. The principle of appetite governing perception applies not only to food but to all human goods and especially to wisdom.
8: ke-tzippor nodedeth min-qinah — “like a bird that wanders from its nest.” The tzippor (bird, H6833) that nodedeth (wanders, flees, H5074) from its qen (nest, H7064) is vulnerable, displaced from its place of safety and belonging. Applied to ken 'ish noded mi-meqomo — “so is a man who wanders from his place.” Maqom (H4725, place, one’s established location in community) as the social nest: the person who wanders from his social-relational home is as exposed as the nestless bird.
9: shemen viqetoret yessammach-lev — “oil and perfume bring joy to the heart.” Shemen (H8081, oil — used in festive anointing and in cooking) and qetoret (H7004, incense/perfume) as the physical means of joy and celebration. Ve-methaq re'ehu me'atzat-nefesh — “and the sweetness of a friend comes from earnest counsel.” Me'atzat nefesh (“from counsel of the soul/self” — genuine counsel that comes from the friend’s whole inner engagement rather than from social performance) is the human analog of sensory pleasures: it makes the heart joyful in the way that fragrance makes the body joyful.
10: re'akha ve-re'a 'avicha 'al-ta'azov — “do not forsake your friend and your father’s friend.” The inherited friend (re'a 'avicha, your father’s friend) is the intergenerational relational capital that connects a person to a community of support. Uve-yom 'edkha 'al-tavo' beit 'achicha — “and do not go to your brother’s house on the day of your trouble.” The proximity-caveat: a distant friend who knows you may be more reliable than a nearby relative who does not. Tov shakhein qarov me-'ach rachok — “better a neighbor who is near than a brother who is far away.”
11: chakham beni ve-ssammach libbi — “be wise, my son, and bring joy to my heart.” The parental investment in the son’s wisdom returns to the paternal-address frame of Proverbs 1-9. Ve-'ashivah chorphi dabar — “so that I may answer him who reproaches me.” The father’s own honor in the community depends in part on the son’s wisdom: the son’s foolishness would be used as reproach against the father. The communal accountability structure of wisdom formation: the teacher’s reputation is implicated in the student’s life.
12: 'arum ra'ah ra'ah venisttah — “the prudent man sees danger and hides himself.” 'Arum (H6175, prudent, shrewd — the same word used of the serpent in Genesis 3:1) paired with ra'ah (saw, perceived the evil) and nisttah (H5641 Niphal, hid himself) is the positive use of the same foresight that protects the 'arum from harm. Upethaim 'avru ve-ne'nashtu — “the simple pass on and are punished.” The simple (pethaim) who see the same danger but do not respond appropriately suffer the consequence. The difference between prudence and simpleness is not intelligence but responsiveness to danger-signals.
13: qach bigdo ki-'arav zar — “take his garment when he has guaranteed for a stranger.” The collateral-seizure instruction (take the pledge-garment, the beged) when someone has co-signed for a foreigner (zar) repeats the warning of 20:16 and Proverbs 6:1-5. The practical wisdom about credit risk: guaranteeing for strangers or foreigners carries a higher default risk than guaranteeing for members of one’s own community where social accountability mechanisms apply.
14: mevareikh re'ehu beqol gadol — “whoever blesses his neighbor with a loud voice early in the morning.” The excessive and poorly-timed blessing (qol gadol, loud voice; baboqer hashkiem, rising early in the morning when people are asleep or barely awake) is identified as a social violation: qillelah techashev-lo — “it will be counted as a curse to him.” Qillelah (H7045, a curse) as the result of poorly-calibrated enthusiasm: the intention does not determine the effect; the social context and timing do. Inappropriate expression of good will can be as harmful as its absence.
15: delet nittefeth be-yom sag-rim — “a continually dripping roof in a day of heavy rain.” Nittaf (H5197, to drop, to drip) characterizes the delet (door/roof, H1817) in the context of steady rain (sag-rim, pouring rain): the leak that cannot be escaped, the persistent irritation that the rain makes worse. Applied to ve-'eshet midyanim nishtavah — “and a quarrelsome wife are alike”: the contentious woman and the leaking roof share the quality of persistent, inescapable irritation. The aphorism is about the structural inevitability of the annoyance, not a moral judgment on the person.
16: tzofeneyha tzafan ru'ach — “to restrain her is to restrain the wind.” The contentious woman of v15 cannot be “restrained” (tzafan, H6845, to hide, to store up, to restrain) any more than wind can be stored. Ve-shemen yemino yiqra' — “and oil in one’s right hand encounters himself” — a difficult clause; the most natural reading suggests that attempting to grasp oil (which flows through the fingers) is the analog of trying to manage the contentious person. The impossibility-metaphors frame the proverb as an observation, not a counsel of despair.
17: barzel be-varzel yad — “iron sharpens iron.” The metallurgical process of sharpening (chad, H2300) requires the hard-on-hard contact of iron (barzel, H1270) striking iron. No softer material could accomplish the sharpening. Ve-'ish yachad penei-re'ehu — “so one man sharpens the face of another.” Penei (face, H6440) as the external presentation of the person — the confrontation of one person’s face (perspective, challenge) with another’s produces the sharpening that neither could achieve alone. The Hebrews 10:24 call to “stir up one another” stands in this tradition.
18: notzer te'enah yo'khal piryah — “whoever tends a fig tree will eat its fruit.” Natsar (H5341, to watch over, guard, keep) applied to the te'enah (fig tree, H8384) captures the patient, sustained attention required to bring a fruit tree to bearing. Ve-shomer 'adonav yekhubbad — “and whoever watches over his master will be honored.” The parallel between agricultural stewardship and vocational faithfulness: the same quality of attentive care (shamer) that produces fruit from the tree produces honor from the master.
19: ka-mayim ha-panim la-fanim — “as in water face reflects face.” The mirror-surface of still water (mayim) reflecting (la-panim) the face (ha-panim) that looks into it: a straightforward optical observation. Applied: ken lev-ha'adam le'adam — “so the heart of a man reflects the man.” The proverb probes the relationship between character and perception: we tend to perceive in others what we carry in ourselves, or alternatively, that people reflect each other through their interactions. The ambiguity is productive: the proverb works in both directions.
20: she'ol va'avaddon lo' tisba'nah — “Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied.” She'ol (H7585, the realm of the dead) and 'Avaddon (H11, the place of destruction — from 'abad, to perish; the angel of the abyss in Revelation 9:11) are personified as mouths that are never full. Ve'einei ha'adam lo' tishba'nah — “and the eyes of man are never satisfied.” The parallel between the appetite of death and the appetite of human eyes ('einayim) is the darkest of the comparisons: human desire has the same insatiable structure as the underworld’s appetite for the dead. Only Revelation 22:4 (“they will see his face”) promises what will finally satisfy the eyes.
21: matzreph lakkeseph ve-kur la-zahav — “the crucible for silver and the furnace for gold.” The metallurgical testing apparatus (matzreph, H4715, crucible) for silver and the kur (H3564, furnace/smelting oven) for gold are the instruments that test the metal’s purity under heat. Applied: ve'ish lemahalalhu — “and a man is tested by his praise.” How a person responds to commendation reveals character as accurately as how a metal responds to heat. The response to praise — whether it inflates pride or is received with equanimity — is the social equivalent of the metallurgical test.
22: im-tiktosh 'et-ha'evil ba-makhtesh — “though you grind a fool in a mortar with a pestle.” Katash (H3806, to pound/crush in a mortar) and makhtesh (H4388, mortar) describe the grinding process that reduces grain to flour. But the fool ('evil) ground with a pestle ('eli, H5940) among the grain will not have his foolishness ('ivvalto) separated out: lo' tasur me'alav 'ivvalto — “his foolishness will not depart from him.” The grinding metaphor, applied to the fool, reveals the intractability of a settled character: external pressure cannot change what has become dispositional.
23: yado' teda' penei tzonecha — “know well the state of your flocks.” The pastoral section vv23-27 shifts from the interpersonal aphorisms to agricultural wisdom. Yado' teda' (H3045, the infinitive absolute emphasizing the thorough knowing) applied to penei tzonecha (the face/condition of your flock) means intimate, attentive knowledge of each animal’s state. Shith libbekha la'adarim — “and give careful attention to your herds.” The doubled imperative (yado' teda' and shith lev) emphasizes that pastoral success requires engaged, not merely supervisory, attention.
24: ki lo' le'olam chason — “for riches do not last forever.” Chason (H2633, treasure, wealth stored up) is not permanent; nor is the crown (ve-'im nezer le-dor va-dor — “and does the crown last to all generations?”). The impermanence of wealth and political power motivates the pastoral care of vv23-27: the flock is a renewable resource that requires attention, while stored wealth dissipates. The agricultural argument for active stewardship over passive wealth-preservation.
25: galah chatzir ve-nir'ah deshe' — “the hay is gone, the new growth appears.” The agricultural cycle of cutting (galah, H1540, the hay is exposed/cut) and renewed growth (deshe', H1877, fresh green growth, the first spring growth) describes the natural replenishment cycle. Ve-ne'esfu 'ishvot harim — “and the herbage of the mountains is gathered.” The seasonal abundance that follows from attention to the cycle is the point: stewardship aligned with natural cycles produces abundance.
26: kevasim li-levushecha — “the lambs will provide your clothing.” The flock (kevasim, H3532, lambs) produces levush (clothing, H3830) — wool that is spun and woven. Ve-ther sadeh mechir — “and the goats the price of a field.” The goats’ productivity provides the capital for land acquisition. The pastoral-household economy of vv26-27 traces the chain from flock to clothing to field-purchase to sustenance: the self-sufficient agricultural household that attends to its animals is the concrete picture of the flourishing life that wisdom produces.
27: ve-day chalevat izzim le-lachmecha — “and there will be enough milk from the goats for your food.” Day (H1767, sufficiency, enough — cf. Proverbs 25:16 daieka) is the wisdom standard for material well-being: not excess, but enough. The chalevat izzim (milk of goats) provides lechem le-veithecha (bread/food for your household) and sustenance for your servant girls (na'arotecha). The pastoral chain concludes with the household staff included in the provision: genuine agricultural stewardship sustains the entire community of dependents, not merely the owner. The concluding image of the well-run pastoral household is Proverbs’ picture of wisdom embodied in material life.
